medications.indication_rationale: Gentle emollient soothes the area; definitive potent topical steroid is a GP/dermatology decision after confirmation.
Potent topical corticosteroids are the mainstay treatment but should be prescribed by the GP/dermatologist after confirming the diagnosis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop rubbing/inspecting the area and apply a plain moisturiser once daily.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Lets the self-induced reactive redness settle and reduces anxiety.

patient_education_points.point: This is a benign, slow autoimmune skin condition (lichen sclerosus) where the skin thins and loses colour — it is not your skin dying and not a spreading infection.
The red ring is from rubbing it and will fade if you leave it alone. Use a gentle moisturiser and stop inspecting it.

See your GP within 2-3 weeks to confirm and discuss a treatment cream. Get seen sooner if the area breaks into a bleeding ulcer, turns black, becomes hot and painful, or you develop a fever; and mention any similar patches in the genital area.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Confirm lichen sclerosus (vs morphea), consider potent topical steroid, and screen for genital involvement.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Ulceration/necrosis, hot painful spreading redness, or fever.

must_include_items: Exclude necrosis/vasculitis/infection (painless, non-ulcerated, blanching rub-erythema) before reassuring
Reassure benign extragenital lichen sclerosus and advise stopping the rubbing
acceptable_alternatives: Dermatology referral/biopsy; potent topical steroid via GP
errors_of_commission: ED escalation for a painless white plaque
Alarming the patient about 'flesh-eating' infection or necrosis
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T1 case: passing requires necrosis/infection screen + benign reassurance + routine confirmation/safety-net. ED over-triage is a failure.
